title: Massive reduction in the backlog but questions over quality of decision making in latest ...
description:
First a decision is taken on whether there are reasonable grounds to believe that a person is a victim of modern slavery (the ‘reasonable grounds decision’).
If there are, the next decision is whether there is sufficient information to determine the person is a victim of modern slavery (the ‘conclusive grounds decision’).
There has been some improvement with conclusive grounds decisions.
The overall proportion of positive conclusive grounds decisions has risen from 56% to 66%.
Delays The average time taken from reasonable grounds to conclusive grounds decisions in 2025 was 372 days, compared to 619 days in 2024.
text:
The government’s position on trafficking claims is that there are too many last-ditch attempts to frustrate removal and that protections need to be cut to make the French ‘one in one out’ deal work. The trafficking statistics for 2025 have been published and referrals to the National Referral Mechanism have hit a new record for the second year running. Overall, the success rates are pretty good and considering the amount of scrutiny each case undergoes, the narrative that the system is being abused doesn’t really hold up.

There has also been a shift in the nationality profile of those being referred, a concerted effort to clear the backlog of cases and another pause on the public order disqualifications policy.

Below we look at referrals, refusals, delays and reconsiderations in more detail.

What is the National Referral Mechanism?

The National Referral Mechanism is the framework for identifying and referring potential victims of modern slavery, which also encompasses human trafficking and forced or compulsory labour. Authorised first responder organisations – such as local authorities, certain NGOs and police forces – can refer potential victims to the National Referral Mechanism.

First a decision is taken on whether there are reasonable grounds to believe that a person is a victim of modern slavery (the ‘reasonable grounds decision’). If there are, the next decision is whether there is sufficient information to determine the person is a victim of modern slavery (the ‘conclusive grounds decision’). Around three quarters of referrals are sent to the Single Competent Authority for consideration. The remainder are handled by the Immigration Enforcement Competent Authority which deals with cases for people subject to immigration control who are detained, those whose asylum claims have been deemed inadmissible and non-detained foreign national offenders.

A person can be disqualified from protection and support on the grounds of bad faith or being deemed a threat to public order.

Referrals

There were a total of 23,411 referrals in 2025, up from 19,117 in 2024 which is a 22% increase. The number of referrals of UK nationals has gone up by 15%. There was a surge in referrals of Eritrean nationals who, after UK nationals, are the second most referred nationality (up 81%) and Somali nationals (up 337%). Albanian nationals have dropped out of the top three nationalities for the first time since 2011.

Exploitation is recorded as having occurred in the UK, overseas or a combination of both. Exploitation reported exclusively overseas has risen to 51% of all referrals (up from 43% in 2024), with Libya remaining by far the most reported overseas location of exploitation with 5,982 reports, up 85%. A United Nations Human Rights report published this week highlights how migrants are routinely rounded up and abducted by criminal trafficking networks, often with ties to the Libyan authorities and criminal networks abroad.

The National Referral Mechanism is not a system that is easy to abuse as individuals must be referred by an authorised first responder organisation; they can’t refer themselves. Government agencies now account for 58% of total referrals (up from 53%), with referrals made by UK Border Force alone nearly tripling.

Referrals from NGOs have dropped slightly, and make up only a small portion of total referrals.

Refusals and disqualifications

Overall, positive outcomes for reasonable grounds decisions have improved a little: from 53% in 2024 to 61% in 2025. This is still well below the pre-2023 level of around 90%, but the trend is moving somewhat in the right direction.

Of the reasonable grounds decisions considered by the Single Competent Authority in 2025, 68% of outcomes were positive; an improvement from 57% in 2024. The proportion of positive decisions made by the Immigration Enforcement Competent Authority remains poor at 37%, which is barely any change from 2024.

There has been some improvement with conclusive grounds decisions. The overall proportion of positive conclusive grounds decisions has risen from 56% to 66%. Significantly, the proportion of positive conclusive grounds decisions made by the Immigration Enforcement Competent Authority has jumped dramatically from 20% in 2024 to 43% in 2025. In Q4 of 2025 alone, the positive decision rate reached 51%.

While the trend across 2025 seems to show improvements, it needs context. The Immigration Enforcement Competent Authority has been clearing a large transfer of legacy cases from before it was even established in 2021 (1,914 were transferred in May 2024 and another 1,844 in October 2024). The improvement may well reflect the profile of these older cases – many of which will have been referred at a time when the reasonable grounds threshold was lower and may have accumulated additional supporting evidence over the years spent waiting for a decision – rather than be related to any genuine improvement in decision-making quality.

There were 297 disqualification requests in 2025, all bar six were on public order grounds. The major development is that public order disqualifications have been paused again as of 23 December 2025.

Delays

The average time taken from reasonable grounds to conclusive grounds decisions in 2025 was 372 days, compared to 619 days in 2024.

The backlog of cases awaiting conclusive grounds decisions has been slashed from 17,168 at the end of 2024 to 5,758 at the end of 2025 (a 66% reduction). This is down from the peak of 29,275 at the end of 2022. The government has pledged to eradicate the backlog by December 2026, which actually appears to be possible given this current trajectory.

The question is whether speed is coming at the expense of quality. The data on reconsiderations suggests it may be.

Reconsiderations

The total number of reconsideration requests has gone up significantly from 1,092 in 2024 to 1,528 in 2025 (a 40% increase).

The success rate of reconsiderations has also increased: 79% of reasonable grounds reconsiderations received a positive outcome (up from 70% in 2024), and 77% of conclusive grounds reconsiderations were positive (up from 68%).

The government has added a caveat that positive reconsideration outcomes don’t necessarily mean the original decision was wrong: “In the majority of cases, additional information is provided to the Home Office which was not made available at the time of the original decision.”

The framing that positive reconsiderations don’t indicate bad original decisions is debatable. When almost four out of five reconsiderations are successful, it’s hard to believe that this is only a problem with the sufficiency of evidence provided at a case’s outset.

If evidence that would have changed the outcome was available but not requested, that is itself a failure of the decision-making process, particularly given that victims of trafficking are, by the nature of their exploitation, unlikely to be in a position to compile and present evidence themselves.

Conclusion

The narrative in the statistics is that there are a record numbers of decisions being issued, a dramatic reduction in the backlog and improved recognition rates. While there are signs of improvement, there are ongoing problems relating to decision quality and whether or not the system can keep up with the number of referrals. The fact that reconsideration requests have risen 40% in a year, with many leading to a positive outcome, suggests that speed is being prioritised over quality and that the problem is getting worse not better. As long as this continues, victims of trafficking will continue to be failed by the mechanism that is supposed to protect them.

name: Human Rights
title: Cortez Masto's ICE navigation welcomed by some reform advocates, panned by others
description:
“Nevadans and Americans across the country are clear they want accountability and transparency from immigration enforcement,” Cortez Masto told the gathering.
The groups Cortez Masto met with Tuesday generally support those proposals, but other organizations think the measures fall short and will fail to protect the public from ICE abuses.
“I’ve heard from a number of people, including in my office in DC,” Cortez Masto said in an interview.
The ACLU has filed numerous lawsuits to understand the scope of immigration enforcement in the state and how federal agents are collaborating with local law enforcement.
When asked about an ongoing lawsuit with the DMV, Cortez Masto said “I think that’s great,” adding that is what they should be doing.
text:
Amid a surge of immigration arrests in Nevada, as well as nationwide, Democratic U.S. Sen. Catherine Cortez Masto wants “common sense solutions” to rein in federal agents to prevent an abuse of power.

Not all immigrant groups and attorneys think solutions backed by Cortez Masto and Senate Democrats, such as requiring body cameras and judicial warrants, are enough to curb aggressive actions from the Department of Homeland Security, in particular U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement.

Some groups are also frustrated about the lack of direct communication with the senator

“We need to look at systemic changes to rein in, quite frankly, just the tyrannical behavior of DHS and ICE and all of the contractors that make the ICE machine possible,” said Leo Murrieta, the Nevada director for Make the Road. “How does she know what people need without listening to the groups and the organizations who are fighting to protect immigrant families day in and day out?”

Cortez Masto, who voted against the recent funding proposal for DHS, spoke Tuesday about the need to rein in ICE and U.S. Customs and Border Protections.

The League of United Latin American Citizens, Culinary Workers Union Local 226, and Nevadans for the Common Good were among groups that joined the senator in speaking out against the spike in immigration enforcement.

“Nevadans and Americans across the country are clear they want accountability and transparency from immigration enforcement,” Cortez Masto told the gathering. “There is a clear role for federal law enforcement to play in going after violent criminals. That’s not what is happening. ICE needs to be reined in.”

There were at least 2,155 immigration arrests in Nevada in the first 10 months of President Donald Trump’s second term, nearly three times the previous year, according to the Deportation Data Project.

Many questions about those arrests, including hundreds involving people with no criminal charges filed against them, have gone unanswered.

Nevada Current reached out to DHS to ask about the increase in arrests in Nevada, how it tracks “pending criminal charges,” and what type of data DHS provides to state and local officials.

After asking for an extension to answer questions, the agency responded several days later not with answers to those questions, but with a boilerplate statement, which reads in full: “Under President Trump’s and Secretary Noem’s leadership, DHS has already removed more than 700,000 illegal aliens. If you break the law, you will face the consequences. Criminal illegal aliens are not welcome in the U.S.”

The escalation of aggressive enforcement, especially the surge in Minneapolis, has led to more people, including Democrats in Congress, calling to abolish ICE outright.

“I don’t want to do away with them,” Cortez Masto said in an interview following Tuesday’s event in Las Vegas, noting ICE also plays a role in preventing domestic terrorism and human trafficking.

“There is traditionally a role for ICE, and we’ve had that in the past,” Cortez Masto said, adding that enforcement should be working with local and state police to “go after violent criminals …in our community.”

The mechanisms she and other Senate Democrats support to curb ICE’s abuses include requiring body cameras, prohibiting racial profiling, preventing agents from wearing masks to conceal their identities, and prohibiting enforcement in sensitive locations like schools, hospitals, and churches, and only allowing apprehensions with judicial warrants issued by a judge, not “administrative” warrants that ICE agents have used to sweep people from their homes.

Her vote in favor of DHS funding hinges on those conditions, she said.

The groups Cortez Masto met with Tuesday generally support those proposals, but other organizations think the measures fall short and will fail to protect the public from ICE abuses.

Athar Haseebullah, the executive director for the ACLU of Nevada, which was not at Tuesday’s meeting with Cortez Masto, characterized the proposals, in particular provisions that mandate warrants, as ineffective.

Requiring judicial warrants before seeking people out and arresting them is “already the state of the law,” Haseebullah said.

Requiring body cameras and restricting face masks are good as far as they go, but nowhere near the scale of reform needed to assure people’s rights and safety is protected from federal agents, advocacy groups say.

If lawmakers want to rein in ICE, they should end its partnership with groups like Palantir, said Murietta with Make the Road, who also did not attend the Tuesday event with Cortez Masto.

The data company has used generative artificial intelligence to aid ICE in immigration enforcement, creating a surveillance state exacerbating fear within and danger to the immigrant community, he said.

If the Senate wanted to “take a bite out of the ICE deportation machine” they would also take more aggressive action toward those that work within the agency, Murrieta added.

“The senator would hear about this if she ever made the time to talk to actual people on the ground,” he said. “I don’t know if the senator is open to having that conversation. I wouldn’t know. The door is open and our members are willing to have a conversation. She is welcome anytime.”

‘She can do better’

Tuesday’s presser comes as more and more groups are demanding action to address the harm enforcement is having on the community.

Speakers told stories of immigration agents following people from churches and students afraid to go to school because their parents might be arrested.

Aggressive enforcement is not only weighing on but threatening the state’s economy.

“Our economy depends on immigrant labor,” said Diana Valles, President of the Culinary Workers Union Local 226. “The Trump administration’s attack on the working people who power our economy and keep our hotels, casinos and cities running must end.”

Cortez Masto said she has met with various organizations including businesses, faith based communities, and law enforcement organizations to better understand the needs of the community and how immigration enforcement is playing out in the state.

“I’ve heard from a number of people, including in my office in DC,” Cortez Masto said in an interview. “You have to remember that the work is in DC. A lot of people come out from Nevada that I visit with on a regular basis, and I have had these conversations with them on their concerns.”

Some organizations within the Nevada Immigration Coalition have said they have struggled to get face time with Cortez Masto. Not everyone feels that the door is always open to voice concerns about her approach to handle DHS and ICE.

“The nation’s first Latina in the United States Senate needs to do a lot better because she is failing our state,” Murrieta said. “I have hope that she can do better. And the door is open.”

Not every Nevadan, including and especially those who are undocumented, would easily fly to DC and meet with the senator, he added.

Even when immigration groups fly to DC in hopes of scheduling time with Cortez Masto, “the best we get is a meeting with a staffer.”

Other members of the Nevada Immigrant Coalition said while they’ve been able to speak with staff of Cortez Masto’s office regularly, they haven’t been able to secure a one-on-one with her.

While he understands it might not be feasible to always meet directly with Cortez Masto, a direct meeting is not an unreasonable request, said Noé Orosco, a coordinator for the Nevada Immigrant Coalition

“We did express that the community has felt like we haven’t seen her in the community,” he said.

Some groups, including the ACLU, said neither Nevada senator has reached out to hear about the impact of aggressive immigration enforcement.

The ACLU has filed numerous lawsuits to understand the scope of immigration enforcement in the state and how federal agents are collaborating with local law enforcement. It recently sued the DMV to access communications with the DHS.

Neither Cortez Masto nor Nevada’s other senator, Democrat Jacky Rosen have contacted the ACLU for information about its legal efforts in Nevada, Haseebullah said. The senators have “not made one call or sent one email to our office, not one single time on any immigration issue,” he said.

In an email, Rosen’s office didn’t directly answer the question about contact with the ACLU but added the office is “in constant communication with the Nevada Immigrant Coalition in northern and southern Nevada, and had multiple meetings regarding immigration enforcement and policy.”

When asked about the lack of communication with the ACLU around work they are doing on immigration Cortez Masto applauded their work but questioned the need to talk to the organization.

“I’m happy to talk with anyone. I’m curious what they want from my office?” she said.

When asked about an ongoing lawsuit with the DMV, Cortez Masto said “I think that’s great,” adding that is what they should be doing. “I guess the point is, what do they want from my office?”

Haseebullah said it’s not what the ACLU, along with other immigration groups, want from her office. It’s what they expect from her.

“As someone who champions Latino issues, we want leadership from her,” he said.

title: Shutdown stalemate deepens as White House, Dems dig in on DHS funding - Politico

text:
DHS already has $170 billion to pay for an expansion of the nation’s border enforcement and deportation infrastructure, thanks to the party-line domestic policy and tax legislation Republicans rammed through last summer.

Democrats, for now, believe they have the political upper hand. They are being encouraged by a memo circulated Wednesday from the left-leaning immigration advocacy organization FWD.us, which argues that voters have an increasingly negative view of Trump’s immigration agenda and do not support more funds for immigration agencies — as an estimated $150 billion from the GOP megabill remains unspent.

The memo also highlights a nationwide survey from Democratic polling firm GBAO of 1,000 registered voters, released Feb. 11, that found that 52 percent of respondents want changes to how immigration agencies operate before funding DHS. And roughly three-in-five voters do not want to spend any additional money on ICE.

“A lot of Democrats I talk to, they say, ‘the public doesn’t want us to give them more money. They have $150 billion, which is totally unprecedented,’” said one of the people granted anonymity to speak candidly. “‘This is on them. We’ll come back in the fall.’”

The DHS appropriations bill proposes an additional $64.4 billion in funding for DHS, including $10 billion for ICE.

The second person familiar with Democrats’ thinking said there’s little chance that Democrats and the White House can agree on meaningful “reforms.” The key, the person said, “is the money.”

If last week offered hints of good-faith discussions, this week suggests the negotiations have stalled: Democrats and the White House have exchanged proposals, but neither side appears willing to budge on core sticking points — including requirements for judicial warrants and a proposed ban on face coverings for federal immigration agents.

“We hope [Democrats] get serious very soon because Americans are going to be impacted by this,” White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt told reporters this week.

The stalemate foreshadows what could be a weekslong logjam. And it all but guarantees Trump will deliver his State of the Union address before Congress next week without a solution to fund the agency, which is at the center of Democrats’ outrage over his aggressive immigration agenda and the killing of two American citizens by federal agents in Minnesota last month.

But Democrats are also aware of the potential political pitfalls in allowing a DHS shutdown to drag on.

TSA is viewed as the greatest pressure point for the left. Airport security screeners won’t miss their first paycheck until mid-March, but if this happens, many may stop showing up to work — causing lines to grow in major hubs across the country, potentially during the peak of spring break travel season.

The White House is facing its own pressure from immigration hawks, who believe the administration’s decision to withdraw federal agents from Minnesota allows Trump officials to avoid caving to major Democratic demands for an overhaul. The administration has said little publicly about areas in which it’s willing to negotiate, but has made clear that Democrats’ push to require judicial rather than administrative warrants is a red line.

“They’ve already recalibrated. I haven’t seen any more flashy operations going in any cities right now,” said Jessica Vaughan, director of policy studies at the Center for Immigration Studies, a group that works to restrict immigration.

She described some of the Democrats’ requests, including a prohibition on face coverings, as “unreasonable” and said there are few demands she can see the administration conceding to unless narrowed to “very specific circumstances.”

The White House, for its part, has already focused its message on political vulnerabilities, pointing to FEMA, the Coast Guard and TSA as agencies that will be harmed because Democrats have “chosen to act against the American people for political reasons.”

“Now we have FEMA workers, the men and women of the United States Coast Guard, men and women of TSA, who keep our airports moving, who will be working without paychecks for no good reason other than the Democrats wanting to pick a fight with Donald Trump,” Leavitt said Wednesday. “And the president thinks that’s irresponsible and despicable. He wants the government to be open.”

Democrats are already discussing ways to flip the script on Republicans, with lawmakers expected to push bills like one sponsored by the top Democrat on the House Appropriations Committee, Rep. Rosa DeLauro of Connecticut, which would fund all DHS agencies aside from ICE and Customs and Border Patrol. Democrats will also try to focus their message on how much money DHS received from the GOP megabill last year, as well as the department’s flexibility with those funds.

House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries previewed this strategy Wednesday, standing in front of a poster board that read “Trump’s big ugly bill cut $1 trillion from Medicaid and gave $75 billion to ICE.”

He also told reporters that if there’s little movement this week, Democrats will begin to explore options for funding other agencies outside of ICE and CBP when Congress returns on Monday.

name: Litigation
title: ICE in Minnesota: Federal judge's contempt ruling highlights Minnesota court frustrations
description:
A federal judge in Minnesota has found a government attorney in contempt for not following court orders, highlighting issues within the U.S. Attorney’s Office.
According to court filings, Soto Jimenez was arrested at work by immigration enforcement agents last month.
Soto Jimenez has lived in the United States since 2018 and has no criminal history or final removal order.
She fined Isihara $500 per day for every day that Soto Jimenez does not have his personal items going forward.
Rare contempt ruling Dig deeper: This contempt ruling is rare, with federal court officials reporting that it is only the sixth time since 2020 that a U.S. District Judge has held a party in contempt in Minnesota.
text:
A federal judge in Minnesota has found a government attorney in contempt for not following court orders, highlighting issues within the U.S. Attorney’s Office.

1,000 habeas cases

What we know:

Judge Laura Provinzino held Special Assistant U.S. Attorney, Matthew Isihara in civil contempt for not complying with her court order in a habeas case involving Rigoberto Soto Jimenez, a Mexican immigrant from Big Lake, Minnesota.

According to court filings, Soto Jimenez was arrested at work by immigration enforcement agents last month.

Soto Jimenez has lived in the United States since 2018 and has no criminal history or final removal order.

His case is one of approximately 1,000 lawsuits filed against the federal government challenging the constitutionality of arrests and detainments during Operation Metro Surge.

Left without his driver’s license

The backstory:

Judge Provinzino ordered his release from the Camp East Montana Detention Facility in El Paso, Texas by Feb. 13.

Her order stated that he was to be released in Minnesota and that the government was to "return all property to him."

ICE met the deadline but released Soto Jimenez in Texas without any of his identification paperwork, including a Minnesota driver’s license and a Mexican consular ID card.

At the Wednesday hearing, Provinzino demanded to know why, nearly a week later, his identification paperwork had not been returned.

Overwhelmed by litigation

What they're saying:

Isihara apologized, explaining that the U.S. Attorney’s Office is currently overwhelmed by the litigation. And with a significant legal staff shortage, his team is struggling to keep up with all the court orders.

"There are enormous volumes of cases coming through," explained Isihara, a military attorney on special assignment with the Department of Justice to handle the crush of habeas cases. "This is one instance where, to be candid, the ball was dropped."

In issuing her ruling, Judge Provinzino said that her decision was to ensure that the government complies with judicial orders. She fined Isihara $500 per day for every day that Soto Jimenez does not have his personal items going forward.

In an update Thursday evening, Soto Jimenez's papers have been returned by Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), which nullified the contempt fines against Isihara.

U.S. Attorney Daniel Rosen was quick to fire back, writing in a statement, "Judge Provinzino’s order is a lawless abuse of judicial power."

Rare contempt ruling

Dig deeper:

This contempt ruling is rare, with federal court officials reporting that it is only the sixth time since 2020 that a U.S. District Judge has held a party in contempt in Minnesota.

Kyle Cheney, a senior legal affairs reporter for Politico, has been tracking the mountain of habeas litigation across the country as the Trump administration ramps up its deportation efforts.

He says he has seen significant frustration from federal judges, including those in Minnesota, over repeated violations of court orders.

And while there have been threats of contempt, he had not seen a single judge punish a government official for noncompliance until Judge Provinzino’s order.

"I think judges may be tired of issuing threats and maybe not feeling like they are being heard," explained Cheney. "Usually, the threats work because if you threaten to hold a government attorney in contempt, it gets them going to resolve whatever the issue is. Judges don't usually issue contempt threats unless things reach a really bad point. And when they do, usually, people rush to resolve the issue before they are held in contempt."
